examples.label: argv test1 — crash on string
examples.description: 1 symbolic argument (8 bytes). The bounded model checker finds the byte that crashes the program.

examples.label: argv test2 — numeric overflow
examples.description: 1 symbolic argument (8 bytes). Triggers a numeric overflow in the program's arithmetic.

examples.label: argv test3 — length-dependent bug
examples.description: 1 symbolic argument (16 bytes). The bug depends on the argument's length / content.

examples.label: argv test4 — two arguments must match
examples.description: Two symbolic arguments (8 bytes each). Bug fires only when argv[1][0]='X' AND argv[2][0]='Y' simultaneously. The model checker finds both.

examples.label: argv test5 — checksum match
examples.description: 1 symbolic argument (8 bytes). Bug fires when the bytes form a specific checksum.

examples.label: selfie example — division by zero
examples.description: selfie's division-by-zero test. No symbolic input; the model checker verifies the program at depth 50.

examples.label: selfie example — simple if/else
examples.description: Plain conditional control flow. Useful for examining how the model encodes branches.

examples.label: selfie example — recursive fibonacci
examples.description: Recursive fibonacci. Useful for seeing how stack frames look in the model.

examples.label: selfie example — memory access
examples.description: Memory-access scenario. Shows how loads and stores appear in the BTOR2 model.

examples.label: tiny — simple assignment + witness
examples.description: Smallest meaningful example. Good first thing to load to see the witness playback.

examples.label: tiny — counter driven by input
examples.description: Counter that advances each step based on a symbolic input bit.

examples.label: tiny — counter (no witness)
examples.description: Bare counter model without a witness. Use this to explore the graph structure.
